ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean mandu or veggie mandu; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302648) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Dumplings) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for Korean vegetable mandu in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mandu or Korean vegetable dumplings.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Veggie Mandu is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 6 medium dumplings, as typically served at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Dumplings, vegetable (steamed) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 18033 (dumpling wrapper) and component estimation for spinach, tofu, shiitake mushroom filling
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Korean veggie mandu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for steamed vegetable dumplings (closest analogous entry: FDC ID 168880, 'Dumplings, vegetable, steamed') and cross-checked against component data for spinach (FDC ID 168462), firm tofu (FDC ID 172475), and shiitake mushroom (FDC ID 169242). Gochujang and garlic aioli contribute minor macro adjustments. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented with University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed wheat-based dumplings / dim sum analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean veggie mandu. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed wheat-flour dumplings / dim sum (GI ~45–52, low category). The wheat-based wrapper dominates the glycemic response; the vegetable and tofu filling lowers the overall GI relative to plain wheat products. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 27.75) / 100 ≈ 13.3 (150 g serving × 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 27.75 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from gochujang, Korean pickles, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 480 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation, as gochujang and pickled vegetables are significant sodium contributors.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index wheat-based wrapper with high-fibre, high-protein vegetable and tofu filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 48 and a glycemic load of ~13 per serving, Veggie Mandu produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; the tofu and spinach filling adds protein and fibre that further attenuate glycaemic impact, making it a suitable occasional choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion is controlled to one serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based filling with beneficial phytonutrients from spinach and shiitake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veggie Mandu is low in saturated fat (~0.9 g per serving) and contains no cholesterol-raising trans fats; the plant-based filling provides beta-glucans from shiitake and antioxidants from spinach, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern when sodium intake is monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veggie Mandu (야채 만두) is a Korean-style vegetarian dumpling filled with a seasoned mixture of spinach, firm tofu, shiitake mushrooms, gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste), and Korean pickled vegetables, served with a garlic aioli dipping sauce. The dumpling wrapper is made from wheat flour and is typically steamed, though pan-frying is a common variant that adds a crisp base. The filling delivers an umami-rich, mildly spicy flavour profile with earthy notes from the shiitake and a slight tang from the pickles. Texturally, the wrapper is soft and slightly chewy, while the filling is tender and moist. Mandu has deep roots in Korean culinary tradition, with origins tracing to Chinese jiaozi introduced during the Goryeo dynasty; the vegetable variant is popular across Korea and in Korean diaspora communities worldwide. Nutritionally, Veggie Mandu provides approximately 130 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of ~5.8 g protein, ~3.5 g fat, and ~18.5 g carbohydrate. The tofu and spinach contribute calcium, iron, folate, and vitamin A. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly option compared to plain wheat products. Sodium is the primary clinical concern due to gochujang and pickled vegetable content. This dish is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to the egg-containing aioli. It is a common appetiser or snack in Korean and Korean-fusion restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in veggie mandu?
What is the GI of Korean dumplings?
Is veggie mandu good for diabetics?
Is veggie mandu high in sodium?
Can I eat mandu if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean veggie mandu was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for steamed vegetable dumplings and component-level data for spinach, tofu, and shiitake mushroom. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat-flour dumplings. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Cuisine reclassified from 'Chinese' (input) to 'Korean' based on the dish name 'Mandu', the Korean-origin filling ingredients (gochujang, Korean pickles), and the restaurant context (Dumpling Darlings). Mandu is a Korean dumpling tradition distinct from Chinese jiaozi, though historically related. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived filling ingredients that contribute B12; tofu and vegetables are naturally zero in vitamin B12 (naturally zero). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation (confirmed zero by ingredient analysis). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this food category and shiitake mushroom vitamin D content is highly variable depending on UV exposure; not reliably estimable. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite food. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database steamed wheat-flour dumpling entries; no direct measurement for Korean veggie mandu exists in the published literature. 7. Garlic aioli is listed as a condiment/dipping sauce and contributes minor fat and sodium to the overall dish; its contribution is partially reflected in the fat and sodium estimates. 8. The dish is served at Dumpling Darlings - Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 9.20 per serving.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Reclassified cuisine from Chinese to Korean based on dish identity (mandu is Korean). Added Korean mandatory local name 야채 만두. Added Chinese script names 素饺子 and 素餃子 as supplementary aliases. Estimated nutrients from USDA FoodData Central steamed vegetable dumpling data. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries. Populated all health context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Confirmed G6 satisfied for Korean cuisine (Korean native-script name present).
